title: Collective neutrino oscillations: Many-body non-forward effects and non-classicality
authors: Julien Froustey, Ermal Rrapaj, Yuhao Liu, Gushu Li, Costin Iancu, Vincenzo Cirigliano
abstract: Neutrino evolution in dense astrophysical environments is typically described either within a quantum kinetic framework, which neglects the build-up of multi-body correlations, or through simplified many-body calculations that allow significant entanglement to develop. In this work, we compare these two approaches in a simple neutrino-gas configuration, with particular emphasis on the role of non-forward scattering processes. These effects are incorporated either through a collision term in the kinetic description, or by considering the full neutrino-neutrino many-body Hamiltonian. We highlight differences between the two descriptions in both their characteristic timescales and asymptotic behavior. Motivated by the natural suitability of quantum computing for many-body calculations, we further investigate the non-classicality of neutrino evolution, discussing Trotter error scaling, along with the associated costs of constructing quantum circuits in terms of entangling gates and non-Clifford gates. We find that the resources needed for neutrino many-body evolution are on the low end of typical high-energy physics problems and on the mid to high end with respect to quantum chemistry problems. For the full Hamiltonian, resource requirements increase relative to the truncated version. We emphasize the importance of efficient fermion-to-qubit encodings, which are essential for reducing the substantial computational resources required for such simulations.
summary: The paper rigorously compares different theoretical approaches—mean-field, Quantum Kinetic Equation, and full many-body Hamiltonian—for modeling neutrino flavor oscillations in dense astrophysical matter. It demonstrates that non-forward scattering effects are crucial, leading to significant deviations from simpler models. Furthermore, it quantifies the quantum computational resources required for these complex simulations by analyzing entanglement growth and Trotter error scaling.
main_problem: The study compares neutrino evolution using mean-field approximations against full many-body treatments, focusing on non-forward effects and quantifying the resulting quantum non-classicality.
main_result: Full many-body calculations show significantly higher entanglement entropy compared to truncated or mean-field results, indicating that multi-body correlations are crucial for accurate dynamics. The resource estimation suggests these simulations require substantial computational resources related to gate complexity.
method: The authors employ both solving differential equations (QKE/Mean-Field) and estimating quantum circuit requirements (Trotter error scaling, gate costs) for the full Hamiltonian evolution.
important_parameters: The comparison hinges on the relative importance of non-forward scattering terms versus those captured by mean-field approximations; resource costs are compared against quantum chemistry problems.
main_assumptions: Key assumptions include using a 'plane waves in a box' discretization for momentum space and employing first-order Trotter error bounds to estimate computational cost.
figures_summary: Figures compare time evolution of occupation numbers across Full, Truncated, and Mean-field Hamiltonians; entanglement entropy plots show deviations from mean-field predictions; and resource estimation figures detail scaling with system size N.
paper_structure: The paper systematically develops the mathematical framework for neutrino self-interactions in various dimensions (1D, 2D, 3D), compares QKE/Mean-Field results against full many-body simulations, and concludes by analyzing quantum computational resource requirements via Trotter error bounds.
why_interesting: This work directly bridges high-energy astrophysics (neutrino oscillations) with cutting-edge quantum computation theory by quantifying the necessary resources to simulate complex interacting quantum systems beyond mean-field approximations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Two-dimensional grid of momenta used in this work. The states initially occupied in our test calculation are shown in blue, while the two-neutrino case shown in red is discussed in Appendix C. The momentum mode ⃗p = ⃗0 is excluded, as we restrict ourselves to ultrarelativistic neutrinos.

FIG. 2. Time evolution of the νe occupation numbers for the initial state (25). Many-body calculations using the full (for- ward/exchange) Hamiltonian are shown with solid (dashed) lines, while the mean-field result is shown with a dotted line. Each panel corresponds to the momentum point (px, py) on the grid shown in Fig. 1.
FIG. 3. Same as Fig. 2, but for the νµ occupation numbers.
FIG. 4. Same as Fig. 2, but for the ντ occupation numbers.
FIG. 5. Evolution of the average kinetic energy for each neu- trino flavor. Plotting conventions are the same as Fig. 2.
